What issue are you running into exactly with quoted keys? As far as I can tell, you can put basically anything you want in your keys except an equals sign.  

[Version 0.3.1](https://github.com/toml-lang/toml/blob/master/versions/en/toml-v0.3.1.md#table) doesn't mention quoted keys. Okay I have now uploaded the changes I did. It currently has also some support for signals but there are still a few questions remaining things. For one you used lowercase names (like `signal`), while the Python convention is to use CamelCase for class names (like `TypedSignal`). I followed the Python convention for now. It is similar with the imports which are alphabetically sorted, then by “type” (first `import X` and then `from X import Y`) lastly by “source” (first stdlib, then 3rd party and then local packages).

I also haven't added any tests for the annotations yet, as they cannot be used together with Python 2, so they need to be separate files. I actually haven't set up pydbus in Python 3 because the default Python is 2.7.

Also the `TypedSignal` cannot use annotations at all and also needs to define the name too. Currently it is all just in one parameter which is directly used in the for loop (so a list of tuples would work). Maybe there are better approaches? It doesn't actually add simple `signals`, but I just wanted to get this revision out first.

I guess you might just have released a 1.1.3 to PPA for which `pew cp` was broken, and 1.1.4 fixes that... in which case, 1.1.4 has already been released over there.

So, this means that it'd be better to release the same versions (both of them) for upstream Pew, and not add any other small change to 1.1.4 (inconsistencies between the same versions could be really confusing).
The version number in GitHub should probably be bumped, but whatever is in GitHub master should considered to be future behavior.

I find this surprising. Every other software I use will fail in one way or another when a disk is full or bytes cannot be flushed to destination.

Maybe I was not clear in explaining the problem and you understood something else.
The problem is that the output of minimap fills the disk but this doesn't result in a failure.
When using minimap in a pipeline, the only way to know if minimap succeeded is to check if the output isn't corrupted which is very unreliable.

As for technically achievable, even plain `cat` does it:
```
# cat /dev/zero > file ; rm -f file
cat: write error: No space left on device
```

XQuartz is fine.  The reason you get this error is because anaconda, for some reason I'll never understand, includes copies of the X11 header files with a bunch of X11 functions removed.  So if your compiler's include search path specifies to look in the anaconda location first then it picks up these bad X11 header files and BOOM, errors about missing X11 functions.  This is especially irritating since the X11 API is ancient, maybe older than me, I forget the exact history.  So I don't see any reasonable excuse for why anaconda would include broken X11 headers.  I usually tell people to delete anaconda at this point.  But maybe you don't want to do that.  

A better and more permanent solution would be to modify the CMake scripts to adjust the compiler include search path to somehow avoid anaconda's X11 files.  You can see where the X11 path is located in dlib/CMakeLists.txt here: https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/CMakeLists.txt#L252.  You can even see that there is a comment there that the current way it does it should avoid anaconda's nonsense, but apparently it doesn't anymore :)

Also, in general, you don't need to set rpath to use the mkl.  This kind of thing is generally handled by some system wide setting like adding the MLK folder to your LD_LIBRARY_PATH or some other system list of folders the runtime linker searches for shared libraries.  I don't recall the setting on the mac.  But on most unix systems it's setting LD_LIBRARY_PATH.  See also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rpath.  So this isn't a dlib issue, just a matter of installing the MKL correctly. 

To summarize, you can use dlib with anaconda.  You just need to make sure the anaconda X11 header files aren't found since they are broken.  If you find an adjustment to cmake that avoids them please do submit a PR :)

Yes, that's the intended behavior -- with pysnmp, user-facing side is always SMIv2. That is why even SNMPv1 TRAPs get translated into SNMPv2 TRAPs. Or when you are sending v1 TRAPs you still need to pass v2 TRAPs to pysnmp.

Why is it a problem? I thought of it as a feature because you get TRAPs uniformly, regardless of SNMP version your peer is talking, no?

You can convert it back to SNMPv1 if you want to:

```
from pysnmp.proto.proxy import rfc2576

v1_pdu = rfc2576.v2ToV1(v2_pdu)

```
Where `v2_pdu` is what you get from low-level pysnmp hooks. Or you could reconstruct it from the var-binds you get in the callback:

```
from pysnmp.proto.proxy import rfc2576
from pysnmp.proto.api import v2c

v2_pdu = v2c.SNMPv2TrapPDU()

v2c.TrapPDUAPI.setVarBinds(v2c_pdu, varBinds)
v1_pdu = rfc2576.v2ToV1(v2_pdu)

```

Hey Vladimir,

Thanks for the kind words - glad you like my approach!

I'll try to answer your questions as best I can - let me know if you have any more questions or if something is not clear.

Q1: This is indeed the case. However, given the type of strategies that I test, I don't think it is really an issue. For example, say you have a monthly rebalancing strategy. On the rebalancing day, you will run the algo a few minutes before the close and execute the necessary trades. Although the price used to calculate the momentum and the trade price might be different, I don't think there will be major discrepancies and nothing would suggest a systematic bias in my favor. Even for daily trades I can't see this being a big issue and as such, I prefer using the current day's price vs. the next day's close. 

Furthermore, I don't think you will have a look-ahead bias because the close price is your entry price. Although it is technically not the price that you can trade at, it is quite near and differences should cancel out over time. Look-ahead bias is more prevalent in some other frameworks where returns are used instead of prices. In this case you cannot use the same day's return to determine algo logic (buy/sell) and calculate the performance. You must take the next day's return as your return. I hope this makes sense...

That being said, if you were to use this framework on an intra-day basis, then this would probably become more of an issue and in that case it would indeed be better to be more conservative and take the next bar's close. Maybe we should add some kind of option to ```Backtest``` that would modulate this behavior?

Q2: Good catch. I had not yet come across a backtest with this need since I mostly focused on ETFs and futures, but this would indeed be an issue with a dead / delisted / bankrupt company. I think this should be relatively easy to implement since we can check if the position is non-zero and act appropriately. I think we still would need to throw some kind of exception if someone tries to initiate a position on a NaN priced security though. 

However, now that I think about it, you could have some tricky bugs here. For example, say you rebalanced at time t and for some reason (exchange closed or something like this) some securities had NaN values on this date only (while others did not). You cannot simply replace NaN for 0 here and assume the company is no longer operational...

I need more time to think this one through - maybe a distinction between NaN and 0 - 0 meaning bankrupt and NaN meaning missing data. This way if we have a non-zero position and a 0 price, we know that the company is bankrupt/removed and we can deal with it. If it is simply missing data, then it might be up to the user to fill the NaNs with appropriate values...

Q3: Very good point once again. I think this should indeed be part of the calculation (and up to the user to use this piece of data or not). Therefore, ```bt.core.StrategyBase.commission_fn``` should accept both quantity and price (we can add price as second argument to avoid breaking existing code). It will then be up to the user to determine how and what to use. I assume slippage could be included in the commission function as well as I don't think a separate function is necessary here. 

Q4: Depends what you need here. As you mentioned, the easiest way would simply be to add some kind of logging algo and record the change in each security's position (if different from 0). That way you could pop this algo in when needed and it wouldn't affect the core implementation's performance when not in use. In my experience, I rarely look at detailed transaction logs - I prefer looking at security weights, for example, to make sure everything is running along smoothly. 

Hope this helps!

Cheers,
Phil

I agree that including items which lack a given attribute in a View or Section which focuses on that attribute is a Good Thing; but I think it is useful primarily when one is in _Review_ mode -- the time you'll ask yourself "Should any items which lack a given attribute (Context, Keyword, or Tag) be assigned one?"

When you want to plow through your _Next Actions_, on the other hand, having the attribute-lacking items at the top of the list will be distracting.  For that reason alone, I recommend returning to the previous practice of assigning either __~\~~__ or the "more informative, but still bottom-sorting" __{None}__ to items which haven't been assigned a value for a particular attribute class.

Not a bug ... feature :-)
While merging **array_of_dicts** it detects a matching key in the first dict and merges them!
So you get the result: first item in list merged and the second one unchanged.

A list of dicts is really a hard topic and I spend quite some time on the merge of this type.
I assume the expected result differs from structure to structure. And indeed, I think this is not a ideal structure to describe data.

For the case you do not like the merge method to be too clever, I have implemented a boolean kwarg **mergelists**. It defaults to true in order to keep the original behavior.

see: examples/issue25.py

`base = """
key_one: 1
key_two: 2
array_of_dicts:
- dict_key_one: a
  dict_key_two: b
  dict_key_three: c
- dict_key_one: a1
  dict_key_two: b1
  dict_key_three: c1
"""
layer = """
key_two: 2222
array_of_dicts:
- dict_key_one: a2
  dict_key_two: b2
  dict_key_three: c2
"""


CONF = hiyapyco.load([base, layer], method=hiyapyco.METHOD_MERGE)
print hiyapyco.dump(CONF)

print "... using mergelists=False ..."
CONF = hiyapyco.load([base, layer], method=hiyapyco.METHOD_MERGE, mergelists=False)
print hiyapyco.dump(CONF)`

this will give you:
`key_one: 1
key_two: 2222
array_of_dicts:
- {dict_key_one: a2, dict_key_two: b2, dict_key_three: c2}
- {dict_key_one: a1, dict_key_two: b1, dict_key_three: c1}

... using mergelists=False ...
key_one: 1
key_two: 2222
array_of_dicts:
- {dict_key_one: a, dict_key_two: b, dict_key_three: c}
- {dict_key_one: a1, dict_key_two: b1, dict_key_three: c1}
- {dict_key_one: a2, dict_key_two: b2, dict_key_three: c2}`

Hope the explanation was somehow clear and the fix helps.

When receiving messages via a socket connection, it's common to receive partial messages when under load.  As these partial messages are appended to the reassembly buffer (using `append_buffer`, you can sometimes get no new FIX message from the appended bytes (and sometimes get multiple FIX messages).

So the recommended structure is to call `get_message` until it returns `None`.

I thought this was better than returning, eg. `StopIteration`, which would make the more usual case where you get one FIX message for each `append_buffer` kinda ugly.

That said, I'm playing with extensions to `get_message` that will do more (and optionally less) validation of the messages when parsing, and it would be possible to add an option to have it raise something rather than return `None`.

What's your use-case?   Why is an exception the best solution for you?  What exception would you expect?

Sorry about that. I'm traveling at the moment, but to give you a quick explanation: the input argument should be a list of integers that gives the column ids of the features you want to propagate. Innternally, a slice  ``X[..., your_list]`` of the input matrix will be propagated to the output matrix.

Importantly, in the output matrix, the propagated features are the **leftmost** columns. So if you pass ``[3, 6, 9]`` as the ``propagate_features`` to layer 1, in the the output matrix of layer 1, these columns will have indices ``[0, 1, 2]``. 

Hope this helps for now, let me know if you have any other questions!

Underscores are quite common for variable names in Python and in my opinion should therefore also be valid for container and client configuration names. As the restriction only seems to apply to the host names but not the Docker container names, I have added a method for replacing certain characters for container host names and link alias names. Underscores are replaced with dashes. Similarly, dots are replaced with dashes since they would lead to a strange subdomain syntax. The behavior can be customized by changing the  `dockermap.map.policy.base.BasePolicy.hostname_replace` dictionary.

While you start and create another thread, it doesn't actually end up helping in this instance because your bot starts **using the other thread** (it's triggered by tk from you pressing the button). This means that your bot and tk event loop are still using the same thread, it's just no longer thread 0 (the default).

To fix this, rather than using a button to start your bot, merely start your bot normally at the bottom of the script after `tk.start()`. This starts tk on a different thread, and starts your bot on the main thread.

It's also worth noting that most asyncio objects are **not threadsafe**, so you will need to look into how to safely interact with asyncio from your tk thread.

You can use [discord.on_ready](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.on_ready).

Here's an example:
```
@bot.event
async def on_ready():
    server = discord.get_server("server id")
    await bot.send_message(server.get_channel("channel id"), "Message content")
```

[`discord.Channel.id`](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.Channel.id) is an attribute, not a method.
The error is telling you that you're unable to call it as such.

For future questions like this, you should join either the [official discord.py server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) or the [Discord API server](https://discord.gg/discord-api) for help, as the README recommends.

http://whitenoise.evans.io/en/stable/changelog.html#simpler-cleaner-django-middleware-integration

In addition, to check whether a user is authenticated, (for Django at least) the WhiteNoise middleware would need to be placed after the Django session/authentication middleware, which makes database requests etc. This would negate the performance benefits of WhiteNoise.

In a [post](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/pydicom/PJ9K37dsmBk/OXLJtprjCQAJ)[1] on the pydicom google group, cardioninja shared some concise code which can decompress 8-bit jpeg, if [libjpeg-turbo](https://sourceforge.net/projects/libjpeg-turbo/) and its cffi interface [jpeg4py](https://github.com/ajkxyz/jpeg4py) are installed.  This was confirmed to work on files from Philips Echocardiography machines - iE33 & Affinity.  Cardioninja also comments that it would be trivial to use PIL or Pillow instead, but libjpeg-turbo was much faster. Cardioninja's code is replicated below.  As a side note, it also uses mmap, which is related to #139.

I think it would be interesting to add "handlers" for various types of compressed images through a plug-in architecture -- in this case, if the code was registered as a handler for 8-bit jpeg, then anyone with the right libraries already installed could transparently read those images.

Handlers should check for their dependencies and only register for transfer syntaxes they can decompress given the libraries installed.  Perhaps a call function could also determine which additional libraries could give more functionality, so that pydicom could point to them in the NotImplemented exception message. (e.g. 'pydicom cannot handle this image, but if you installed `<name/hyperlink>` then it should be able to via plugin `<plugin-name>`').  If multiple handlers could process the file, the message would list all such options, so that the user could choose the easiest one to install for their platform.  Such a plugin framework could also work for @Korijn's gdcm-calling code.

Here is cardioninja's 8-bit jpeg code:
```python
import os
import sys
import mmap

import numpy as np

import jpeg4py

def jpeg_extract(file_fl):
    frames = []
    with open(file_fl, "r+b") as dicom_f:
        with mmap.mmap(dicom_f.fileno(), 0) as dicom_m:
            i = 0
            while 1:
                start = dicom_m.find(b"\xff\xd8\xff\xe0", i)
                if start == -1:
                    break
                i = start
                end = dicom_m.find(b"\xff\xd9", i)
                if end == -1:
                    break
                try:
                    jpg_data = np.fromstring(dicom_m[start: end+2], dtype=np.uint8)
                    jpeg4py.JPEG.clear() #needed as if it ever fails, it won't decode another frame without a reset
                    image_data = jpeg4py.JPEG(jpg_data.copy()).decode()
                    frames.append(image_data)
                except Exception as e:
                    print(e)
                i = end
    return np.array(frames)
```

Perhaps I should add some detection to see if the conflicting version is installed. It's a bit unfortunate that @jsocol and I chose the same name when starting the packages but nearly impossible to change that now since many projects depend on either of the packages.

One option could be to create a compatibility layer so they function with the same API. Although I would argue that my API is currently more Pythonic. To illustrate:
```
>>> import statsd
>>> client = statsd.Client('foo')
>>> counter = client.get_counter('bar')
>>> counter += 10
```
Versus:
```
>>> import statsd
>>> client = statsd.StatsClient(prefix='foo')
>>> client.incr('bar', 10)
```

Or the more common case:
```
>>> import statsd
>>> counter = statsd.Counter('foo')
>>> counter += 10
```
Versus:
```
>>> import statsd
>>> client = statsd.StatsClient()
>>> client.incr('foo', 10)
```

@ckcollab Imo leaving `pytest-spec` wouldn't be that big of an issue:
1. If you don't use the `spec` plugin, test names don't get displayed when running the tests
2. Using `Test`/`test_` *pre*fixes [are a convention](http://doc.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html#test-discovery) anyway
3. `TestTheLoginPage` vs e.g. `TestLoginPage` isn't that big a deal to me ;)

But yeah, to display correct sentences, you'd probably have to rename your test classes as well as your methods. I think once you get used to it, it reads pretty nicely even in the code, though :) As a plus, trying to make the test names fit into a sentence is a neat convention that in my experience helps to think about what exactly you are trying to test. Just my 2ct, though :)

I personally wouldn't hardcode the `The` into the format string. It kinda prevents non-english test names or even usage of  `A`/`An` (e.g. when testing models: `A user... can change her password`). Plus it's then no longer compatible with the behavior of the "original". However, with the config format you suggested, it'd be up to each project to decide on how to combine the format string with the class name :)

A module that does not contain:

* Direct data definitions (e.g., `container`, `leaf`, `list`, `leaf-list`) at the root of the module
* A `uses` statement at the root of the module.

Does not create any data nodes itself, and therefore pyangbind will not create any classes for it. Logical entities are not instantiated in the output classes. To get output classes you need to also include the module that is being augmented - such that those data nodes that are augmented have somewhere to be created in the data tree.

You should include the module that you're augmenting  - i.e., `acme-system` - to get output classes.

from django.core import management

Regarding your last question, `from .MyAdapter import MyAdapter` is not required to be added to the `__init__.py` file.

Adding it to `__init__.py` is just a Python convention that allows you to import the class like this:

```python
from myadapters import MyAdapter
```
 instead of like this:

```python
from myadapters.logic.my_adapter import MyAdapter

It's a limitation of TupleLens that you can't pass it a traversal since it does an implicit `get_monoid` as part of its current implementation. This may be fixable, Ill give it some thought.

In the mean time, you can work around this by extracting the `each_` from out of the `tuple_`:

    right = lens()[1].iso_(lambda a: [a], lambda a: a[0])
    lens([[1,3], 4]).tuple_(lens()[0], right).each_().each_().get_all()

This works by constructing a tuple of two lists `([1, 3], [4])` which can then be eached over twice.

This is a simple example which I have used for a while to reconnect, it closes the particular socket after 3 messages are received and then reconnects.

```
from binance.client import Client

from binance.websockets import BinanceSocketManager

client = Client("", "")


def process_trade_message(message):
    global count, conn_key
    count = count + 1
    print("got ticker message:{}:{}".format(count, message))
    if count >= 3:
        print('closing socket')
        # use either stop_socket or close, or both
        bm.stop_socket(conn_key)
        bm.close()
        # restarting the socket
        conn_key = bm.start_trade_socket('BNBBTC', process_trade_message)
        # reset the count
        count = 0


bm = BinanceSocketManager(client)
count = 0
conn_key = bm.start_trade_socket('BNBBTC', process_trade_message)

bm.start()
```

Server:

```python
import zmq

context = zmq.Context()

    message = socket.recv()
    print("Received request: %s" % message)
    time.sleep(1)
    socket.send(b"World")
```

Client:

```python
import zmq
import time
context = zmq.Context()
socket = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
socket.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:49018;127.0.0.1:49017')
while True:
    socket.send(b"Hello")
    print("Sent request.")
    time.sleep(1)
    message = socket.recv()

This issue blocks the usage of Splinter in environments that require the use of an HTTP proxy. This appears to be a long-standing issue, is there a known workaround?

This script is used in the following scenarios on a headless CentOS 7 server with XVFB, Firefox 38 and Splinter 0.7.2. tcpdump running to to analyze its behavior:
```
from splinter.browser import Browser

fx_config = {
#    'network.proxy.type': 1,
#    'network.proxy.http': 'internal.proxy.local',
#    'network.proxy.http_port': 8080,
    'browser.startup.homepage': 'http://www.example-site-1.net/',
}

browser = Browser('firefox', profile_preferences=fx_config)
browser.visit('http://www.example-site-2.net/')
```

### Baseline:
Without an http_proxy env variable set the browser can be seen resolving site 1 and site 2 DNS names and attempting to open HTTP connections to both of them (along with some Mozilla services), all of which will fail due to the restrictive network environment, eventually raising "socket.error: [Errno 101] Network is unreachable".

### http_proxy env variable set:
When setting the http_proxy env variable and running the script the HTTP proxy DNS name is looked up the browser opens a connection to the server on the proxy port. No connections to site 1 is seen. After this, the site 2 DNS name is resolved and the browser attempts to connect to directly to it. This too fails.

### Firefox proxy settings applied:
When uncommenting the network.proxy settings in the script, settings the Firefox HTTP proxy preferences as has previously been suggested in this thread, the behavior appears identical to the baseline, regardless of whether the http_proxy env variable is set or not. I'm not certain if these settings are correct, but they do match the changes found under about:config when I setup and successfully use Firefox with the same proxy server via the UI on my desktop machine.

To summarize, it appears the browser.visit method does not respect the http_proxy env variable like other connections Firefox opens when used with Splinter. In addition it appears that setting network.proxy settings via Splinter breaks HTTP proxy browsing further, attempting to use direct connections both with and without the browser.visit call.

Hi! We are in the middle of switching to a new automated process which is why we disabled the previous automated update. I will put up the new protocol tonight PST and also push the updated package to pypi.

It was perhaps to get the JSON output looking nicer?

There are pros and cons to filtering the JSON events through PrettyPrinter

Pros: 
- Event delimiters (single quote)
- Simpler to implement

Cons: 
- Harder to read
- Extra cruft in stream
- Slower
- Potential for weird Python string shenanigans?

I did a merge of the JSON changes and setup a more general filter chain in the CLI so you just opt into the output/stats required by the command line arguments. 

https://github.com/jrepp/s2protocol/tree/json-merge

I'm going to read up on what other people are doing for very long JSON event streams. It would be good to use a more standard approach.

I think encoding is something that probably deserves a little bit more thought as well. JSON supports Unicode in the spec (json.org) through the usage of escape characters so that a JSON document can be transmitted as ASCII and compatible with the lowest common denominator of HTTP (AFAIK).  I think using JSON as a textual output has a lot of benefits for connecting command line tools as well because of this.

The problems people are seeing may occur when you decode a JSON document into a python object and then pretty print that python object. Python 2 and 3 represent strings differently and both have special considerations when dealing with Unicode. Sometimes you'll see this as u'my string' I think.

My initial thoughts are that the CLI should ouput JSON text directly, encoded as an ASCII JSON document with no extra conversion. The consuming language should then be able to convert strings containing UTF  according to the JSON spec. ISO-8859-1 is a default ASCII encoding for HTTP and covers the requirements for JSON so I think that should work great.

This should allow things like s2protocol.py | csharptool.exe etc.

Thanks for the feedback. I know this is a small part of an overall larger chain of things and I want to be sensitive to breaking things that have been working for a long time.

I'll make sure to stage the release of these changes - for SC2 3.10 and 3.11 I'll do a dual release by hand.. basically business as usual for the protocol<ver>.py update in master but then I hand merge in a staging branch. This gives everyone using this library a good amount of time to test the changes.

Setuptools 34 specifically drops support for upgrading itself. I wouldn't expect `easy_install -U setuptools` to work unless `six>=1.6` and late `appdirs` and `packaging` are already installed (see setup.py for specific dependencies. Before 34.0.3, it required `six>=1.10.0`. Using `pip` is the best approach.

> [the change causes] `resources_isdir('foo/')` to return True where previously it returned False for anything ending with a backslash.

When phrased that way, it sounds like `resources_isdir` had a bug, and `nose2` happened to be relying on that bug. I can't see any way of interpreting #1232 as anything short of a bugfix.

> The behavior of `resource_listdir()` in eggs is kind of surprising: It returns a list of filenames, and one of those filenames is the empty string. From what I can tell, it's had this behavior since at least setuptools 5.0.
> ...
>  If you want me to change the behavior of `resource_listdir` to be a little less surprising, I could that instead, but there is undoubtedly tons of code that expects the old behavior.

Yeah, that definitely qualifies as surprising. I'd call it a bug.

While I'm loathe to advocate changing something that will cause breakage downstream, this is probably already broken for a lot of packages which don't know it.
A lot of maintainers probably assume that `resource_listdir` behaves the same on zipped and unzipped eggs, and don't explicitly test both cases.

Lots of people probably `pip install -U setuptools` in their builds, but IMO that doesn't make it right to never fix this.
I think the best thing to do is to change it and release it as a major version update to setuptools (`39.0.0`). Optionally, there can be a deprecation+warning cycle for the current behavior, but I'm not sure that's worth the effort for a moderately obscure bug.

fastentrypoints is really just a hack-around for situations where you're not using wheels, i.e. for development testing (something installed with `pip install --user -e`), or if someone else is creating a package and you don't have control over whether they use wheel or setup.py (many Linux distros have scripts for automatically generating their package format from Python packages that, unfortunately, don't use wheel).

For uploading to pypi, you should already be building with wheel, which solves the same problem (I even stole the script fastentrypoints generates directly from wheel... Or maybe one of the related packages, possibly distlib...).

I definitely think of fastentrypoints as a sort of stop-gap until someone fixes this broken packaging system for real.

If I understood correctly this feature could be useful for implementing things like:
```ini
...
ext_modules = cytonize: file.pyx
...
```
or
```ini
[options.ext_modules.cytonize]
primes = 
    sources = primes.pyx
    include_dirs = [...]
    libraries = [...]
    library_dirs = [...]
```
right?

If that is the case, I think the feature would be very desirable... Even though it is very straightforward doing extensions directly in `setup.py`, information about the package cannot be easily extracted from it without executing the script. Since `setup.cfg` is just a plain serialized data structure, it is much easier to parse, and therefore can be used by external tools for several purposes...

Additionally, it allows offloading some responsibilities from the setuptools core. In the aforementioned example, implementing the (de)serialization of the extension descriptor objects to be stored in `setup.cfg` could be the responsibility of a third-party package, or even Cython itself.

Another example I could think of is:
```ini
long_description = orgmode: README.org
```

I hope this helps to clarify the scenario where this kind of pluggable hook could be used.

Ok, so this are the purposed changes. (I'll clean up the commit noise and Silvester's suggestions later). Note that I'm talking about fresh installs here, neither having runtime or build requirements met.

### Changes in skimage
1. Now requires scipy, numpy and dask[array].
2. Removed some magic in setup.py.
3. Gracefully fail when running setup.py and missing numpy.

The effect is that when scikit-image is installed from binaries (read wheel, that is OS X as of now) and setup.py is not ran, dependencies will be correctly installed.

For the source package, it's not the same story, as it will always run setup.py (and fail), never getting to `install_requires`. Though, if pip are able to run setup.py, it can resolve runtime dependencies (which are overlapping with build dependencies) and build dependencies (setup_requires, a setuptools easy_install directive). So we put build dependencies which do not overlap with runtime dependencies (cython), in `setup_requires`.

### Changes in both test environments
1. pip now need `--trusted-host` to install wheels from http

### Changes in travis
1. Test minimum requirements
  - To not tamper with requirements.txt in other cases
2. Only install binaries from wheel house
  - Let setup.py/install_requires sort out the rest
3. Do not use venv (if I can get it to pass without)

### Changes to appveyor
1. Do not package bdist_wininst

    except JavaException:
        pass

In order to report a statistic on a strategy, it should be
- economically meaningful, and
- comparable across strategies

The current form of `information_ratio` is neither (except in perhaps some very narrowly contrived situations). It should be removed or changed. In the absence of a full implementation vis a via Qian and Hua, we could make a simple change that would improve things:

The current definition is in the form:

adjusted_returns = strategy_returns - benchmark_returns
active_risk = std(adjusted_returns)
IR = adjusted_returns / active_risk

This could be changed to:

adjusted_returns = strategy_returns - *beta* * benchmark_returns
active_risk = std(adjusted_returns)

IR = adjusted_returns / active_risk

Hi
I managed to do file uploads with form-data in the following way.
Of course this uploads the file as is and says nothing about the structure or contents of the file.
Your controller needs to know how to handle the contents.
```
/student
      post:
         description: "Add a new student"
         operationId: "student_post"
         consumes:
         - "multipart/form-data"
         parameters:
         - name: file_to_upload
            in: formData
            description: The file to be uploaded
            type: file
            required: true
```

Then in your controller you can access the file via:
```
def student_post():
      file_to_upload = connexion.request.files['file_to_upload']
```

Using the Jinja includes will definitely break editors etc. My preferred solution would be to use the `$ref`s as defined by the Swagger spec, but Connexion doesn't support that yet (and I'm pretty certain it's non-trivial to add).

This PR is intended a stop-gap solution until Connexion has full support for the spec.

You can see how I did it in [turnstile](https://github.com/zalando/turnstile/blob/master/turnstile/checks/__init__.py#L76), you can add entry points in the [setup.py](https://github.com/zalando/turnstile-codevalidator/blob/master/setup.py#L52).

There is no relation between the version of this package and systemd. They just started at the same version after being split, and they are still in similar range.

At the source level, python-systemd should work with any systemd version. (Probably not very old ones, but anything from the last few years). When the C module is compiled, it detects which functions in `libsystemd.so` are available, and links to them. So once you compile against a specific so-version, the module cannot be used with lower version (the dynamic linking will fail). It can be used with *newer* so-versions.

I guess this should be documented somewhere.

```

will silently do the equivalent of transposing the numpy array, then reshaping it again:
```
>>> p.batch([8]).npvalue()

The value of variable regionCount is taken from the variation region list, while subtable->regionCount is the number of region indices referenced by a subtable, which may be less than regionCount. This field in itemVariationDataSubtable struct is better to be renamed to "regionIndexCount" to avoid confusion actually, the name used in OT spec for this data.

Thanks for the feedback and alternate implementation.

I suspect this alternative implementation won't quite meet our needs. I started with an implementation similar to the one you've implemented, but it fails to meet our needs in a couple of ways.

First, I want to be able to implement the CSV processing as an iterable, because it's not clear yet what the use case will be for updating Crunch. While we're working with bulk uploads now, we may want to make incremental (single row) or semi-batched updates. Therefore, I was working to create a CSV handler that could work with the more fundamental iterable model rather than assuming a file stream. Using an iterable model also makes it easier to test and troubleshoot the stream and opens a wide world of other possibilities (such as those found in itertools).

Second, the reason the CSV processing was assuming a string was because I found that streaming data to Crunch is not allowed, that the content length must be declared in the post. I can see how requests might be able to pull the content length from a BytesIO object or on-disk stream, but it won't work for streams in general (and specifically those that can't seek). The call that performs the upload should enforce this constraint early (as long as the constraint exists at the server).

Still, these are just concerns in my mind. I'll need to look at the specifics before I make a more concrete suggestion. In the meantime, I'll close this PR.

Finally got it working! In my SAM template:
```
Api:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
  Properties:
    StageName: Latest
    DefinitionBody:
      Fn::Transform:
        Name: AWS::Include
        Parameters:
          Location: !Sub s3://${ArtifactsBucket}/swagger.yaml
```
where `ArtifactsBucket` refers to the bucket where I upload my Swagger spec prior to 'packaging' the SAM template. Then, in the Swagger template itself I can use the intrinsics, e.g.
```
x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
  type: aws_proxy
  httpMethod: POST
  uri:
    Fn::Sub: arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${GetProjects.Arn}/invocations
```
I'm using the long `Fn::Sub` notation here instead of just `!Sub`, because Swagger doesn't natively support the latter, and also because the docs on `AWS::Include` Transform say that shorthand forms are not supported yet.

On a side note, just wasted ~4 hours troubleshooting the `Invalid ARN specified in the request` error, thinking that `Fn::Sub` for function ARNs doesn't work. Turns out, I only forgot to prepend an ARN reference for `authorizerCredentials` role in the spec (I'm using a custom authorizer). I really wish API Gateway import errors were more informative! Like specifying the concrete line that fails.. In any case, just make sure to prepend `Fn::Sub`/`Fn::Ref`/`Fn::GetAtt` to ALL of your CloudFormation references in the spec.

The only type of thread that's used is `InfiniteLoopThread`, which does this:

```python
class InfiniteLoopThread(BaseCloseableThread):
    """A class that runs a given function an infinite number of times, until told to shut down."""

    def __init__(self, func, thread_name, wait_between_runs=1):
        """
        Run the given func continuously until either shutdown_event is set, or the python interpreter exits.
        :param func: the function to run. This should accept no arguments.
        :param thread_name: the name of the thread to use during logging and debugging
        :param wait_between_runs: how many seconds to wait in between func calls.
        """
        self.shutdown_event = threading.Event()
        super(InfiniteLoopThread, self).__init__(thread_name=thread_name, shutdown_event=self.shutdown_event)

    # ...

    def stop(self):
        self.shutdown_event.set()
```

In any case, I kind of lost track if this was a problem caused by `self.shutdown_event` being dereferenced.. there's a bunch of stuff going on. The main thing is just that something isn't threadsafe and is destroyed and then stale references are accessed.

I wouldn't worry too much about it if it was during system shutdown and processes weren't expected to shutdown cleanly.. then you often get varying and un-reproducable None type references.

However, since it happens during startup, I think there's something more critical that isn't threadsafe.

Agreed - I think in the end we have one primary goal:

***When a user reports a bug with some version info, we should be able to unambiguously map the version number to a particular commit hash.***

There are other nice-to-haves, but this is the most important thing.

When we initially decided to remove version information from being hard-coded in the repo, the main justifications were:

* To avoid multiple sources of truth. Git (in particular a commit hash) is the only thing that can uniquely describe a state of the code, so versioning should be tied to that alone.
* To avoid git commits in our repo like “updated version number”:
  * tend to cause issues e.g. when synced from release branches back to develop
  * needing to re-tag just because of version-change commit

At a high level, here's my understanding of the current situation:

1. Builds are currently broken when run from outside a git repo because we rely on git information (and the `git` command) to extract version info.
2. For "non tag" builds, we try to set the version as the "nearest tag", plus some extra information (e.g. commit hash). However this sometimes outputs misleading version numbers because of the behavior of `git describe`.

For (1), without git information we really know nothing about the state of the code so it might be misleading to "fall back" on a version file. Perhaps for these cases, we should just build with version `0.0.0` or something similarly ambiguous?

For (2), these are developer builds. Perhaps we should avoid trying to infer information about versioning, and simply version them with `0.0.0-commithash` or something similar?

I don't believe re-introducing version information to our code helps us achieve the goal of mapping version number to git commits, because it's trivial for that version info to get out of sync.

Sorry, I cut-n-paste the wrong one, and I probably didn't explain it well. it's not the spaces in the windows file name, it's that 'java' and -D...' are 2 separate list entries. There white space between them. And it's not an issue with how this is in the config definition. It's building the parm list for the execv where this is an issue.
So, instead of this
```
winlocal = {"java" : "java -Dcom.sas.iom.orb.brg.zeroConfigWorkspaceServer.sascmd=C:\\PROGRA~1\\SASHome\\SASFOU~1\\9.4\\SAS.EXE", "encoding" : "windows-1252", "classpath" : cpW }
```
try
```
winlocal = {"java" : ["java", "-Dcom.sas.iom.orb.brg.zeroConfigWorkspaceServer.sascmd=C:\\PROGRA~1\\SASHome\\SASFOU~1\\9.4\\SAS.EXE"], "encoding" : "windows-1252", "classpath" : cpW }
```
But, actually, I don't think this will even happen to work, as the 'java' value is passed as the program name, which won't work. You just won't get away with adding -D things onto the java key. 


Here's a better example of what I was talking about:
If you wanted to pass the following parm
```
-host localhost
```
to a program you execute from python, you build out a parm list. That list would need to be
```
["-host", "localhost"]
```
if you tried to use this instead, it won't succeed
```
["-host localhost"]
```
That's what I was trying to work around. BUT the OS error you got (file not found) is due to the program name that was being given was "java -D...' and there's no file named that. So, it wasn't even getting to the parms issue I was trying to work around. 

When I add in a javaparms key, then you can have this bit in a config def:
```
'java'      : 'java',
'javaparms' : ["-Done", "-Dtwo", "-Dthree"]
```
but it you try to do this, it will not work:
```
'java'      : 'java',
'javaparms' : ["-Done -Dtwo -Dthree"]
or
'javaparms' : "-Done -Dtwo -Dthree"
```

```
from docker.client import Client
from docker.utils import kwargs_from_env
cli = Client(**kwargs_from_env())

print kwargs_from_env()
name_one = 'exec_container'

cli.create_container(**{'name': name_one, 'image': 'golang'})
cli.start(name_one)
cli.logs(name_one, stdout=True, stderr=True)

cli2 = Client(**kwargs_from_env())

e = cli2.exec_create(container=name_one, cmd='ls /usr/local/bin')
print cli2.exec_start(exec_id=e['Id'])

```

music21 can represent multidurational chords:

```
c = note.Note('C4')
c.duration.type = 'half'
e = note.Note('E4')
g = note.Note('G4')
ch = chord.Chord([c e g])
```

but such chords do not import/export to musicxml.  In creating such a feature, it's helpful to know that many musicxml readers expect [though the spec does not require it] chords to be represented from lowest note to highest note.  Thus the specification that the longest duration must come first may be incompatible with implementations expecting lowest note to come first (in this example, the lowest note is the longest, but that's the opposite of what we see in, say, Bach solo violin partitas).  This makes the export implementation tricky in that we should make sure to order first by duration and second by pitch (lowest written to highest written) and in such a way that speed is not greatly compromised for the vastly more common case of chord factors all having the same duration.

Happy to take a patch/pull request for this -- given the todo list length and my new position outside of music dept. for the next three years, I don't think that it's something I'll be getting to soon.

I don't know of a truly simple way of doing this.  I actually dread working on Conda because it is so easy to get mixed up.  I hope we can fix this by making Conda more independent of the root environment into which it is installed.  Here's what I do:

1. Install a separate miniconda installation, just in case things go wrong (or work with a docker image or VM)
2. ```conda install setuptools``` (because it's not installed by default and conda needs it to install itself
3. manually delete the site-packages/conda folder and site-packages conda-3.xx.x egg info file (or else you might get the release version overriding your dev version.)
4. ```pip install -e .``` in the conda folder

Note that shell scripts do not get updated like Python files do with ```pip install -e .```  If you're tweaking shell scripts, you need to re-run ```pip install -e .``` to have conda "see" the updates.  You  should see output when running ```pip install -e .``` that it is copying the shell scripts.  You should be suspicious if you change the shell scripts, run ```pip install -e .``` and do not see the scripts mentioned.  It will not mention them if they haven't changed.

All of this could be automated pretty well with a Docker image, I think, so that you could develop on your machine, and never have to worry about an installation on your local machine.  I'll chew on that.

Any other suggestions welcome.

For all of our CI, the git tag is the source of truth for version information rather than something hard-coded in a file somewhere. 

Github does not associate the git tag with the release tarbal. 

So yes, you need to provide this information another way. And that other way is writing the version to the .version file.

Of course our own preferred way is to use git to clone the repo (even if a shallow clone)  at the point of the git tag version you want to build.

Hey guys,
there is one last thing I would like to discuss in this context. Earlier in the PR, I made the suggestion to move the channel prioritization (not version or build) upwards in the solver code, essentially before the feature minimization, see [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/6296#discussion_r152991684).
While this does not solve issues within a channel, it makes it easier to obtain a consistent behavior for given channel priorities. The effect would be that feature minimization would never overrule channel priorities. Everybody I have spoken with told me so far that this is what he would have expected anyway. This is also supported by the wording in [the docs](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-channels.html?highlight=priority#after-conda-4-1-0) which clearly say that this is what happens first.

Would it be ok to have this change in addition to this PR, but by default instead of optional?

Unfortunately, I think it depends on what process starts bash.  In my testing with MSYS2 and old-ish git bash (2.6.4), having the .exe file extension worked.  This stuff is all way too complicated...

At any rate, if this makes things work better for you, I'm +1 for merging.

Yes, this is for installing menu shortcuts.  Shortcuts for all users requires admin elevation.  There's no way around that.

You could create a file called ".nonadmin" in your C:\Miniconda3 folder.  That will trick the menu installation to happen for only the current user.  Any menus created for environments thereafter will be local to that single user.

This is a really fragile way to operate, though.  You will have major permission issues if more than one person tries to use conda at once, and if people are using an environment when someone else changes it, then again, you'll get permissions errors and an upredictable broken final state.

DIsk space is cheap.  I would strongly advise you to give everyone their own miniconda installation.  If you need to preserve bandwidth, I would recommend caching packages to a local proxy as people download them, and have your many installed minicondas all go look for packages at the proxy rather than directly on the internet.
See https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2502#issuecomment-395490219, #7194, and related.

Deleting one by one would not be a problem.  If it is it _really_ indicates something wrong with your cluster.

Deleting _en masse_ is what's enabled by default now, as doing it one by one actually can cause more headache for the cluster, as it needs to figure out how to rebalance shards.  If it takes more time for you to get a sane cluster state, then a higher timeout should be the solution.  

This _may_ have to do with your refresh interval.  This interval is actually counter-productive.  On of our top Lucene committers did some performance tests and found that refresh intervals longer than 10s did not help, and actually slowed things down.  The sweet spot is between 1s and 10s in nearly every scenario he created, at high and low indexing volumes.  I recommend going back to at least 10s, if not the 5s that Logstash defaults to.

A patch would be most welcome. I think the proper way to implement this would be very similar to how we handle `ignore` and `request_timeout` parameters in `Transport`.

The most important thing is to formalize the REST API and version it
properly. Kyle - thanks for jumping into that side of the discussion.

I agree there are pros/cons of developing it separately or within
jupyter/notebook. I am open to both, with the only comment that if the
updated REST API is released in jupyter/notebook, it should follow the
semantic versioning of the notebook. It is hard to get a REST API right the
first time, so this necessarily limits your ability to iterate as quickly
as you might like.

I am not in favor of having of a optional header that allows us to avoid
formalizing and versioning our core REST APIs.

However, we might want to begin to version our REST APIs separately from
the jupyter/notebook package version. We do have different REST APIs and
having a separate version for each would allow us to more communicate to
users the nature of a given server on a more fine grained basis. Splitting
the notebook server out from the UI would also help in this respect.

import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd.core import primitive
from autograd import grad

class A(object):
    @primitive
    def f(self, x):
        return x**2

    f.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, self, x: 2.*x, argnum=1)

1. create a file name tinode and paste the below code, alter the code to match your environme
```
#!/bin/sh

SCRIPT="/go/bin/server" # or $GOBIN/server
RUNAS=root # user 
NAME="tinode" # app name

PIDFILE="/var/run/$NAME.pid"
LOGFILE="/var/log/$NAME/daemon.log"
CONFIG="$GOPATH/src/github.com/tinode/chat/server/tinode.conf"

start() {
  # check if service already running, exit if service is running
  if [ -f $PIDFILE ] && [ -s $PIDFILE ] && kill -0 $(cat $PIDFILE); then
    echo 'Service already running' >&2
    return 1
  fi

  # reload environment variables, this is not neccessary
  source /root/.bash_profile

  echo 'Starting service?~@?' >&2
  local CMD="$SCRIPT --data=$CONFIG &> \"$LOGFILE\" & echo \$!"
  su -c "$CMD" $RUNAS > "$PIDFILE"  # runs command and get PID
 # Try with this command line instead of above if not workable
 # su -s /bin/sh $RUNAS -c "$CMD" > "$PIDFILE"

  sleep 2
  PID=$(cat $PIDFILE)
    if pgrep -u $RUNAS -f $NAME > /dev/null
    then
      echo "$NAME is now running, the PID is $PID"
    else
      echo ''
      echo "Error! Could not start $NAME!"
    fi
}

stop() {
  if [ ! -f "$PIDFILE" ] || ! kill -0 $(cat "$PIDFILE"); then
    echo 'Service not running' >&2
    return 1
  fi
  echo 'Stopping service?~@?' >&2
  kill -15 $(cat "$PIDFILE") && rm -f "$PIDFILE"
  echo 'Service stopped' >&2
}

uninstall() {
  echo -n "Are you really sure you want to uninstall this service? That cannot be undone. [yes|No] "
  local SURE
  read SURE
  if [ "$SURE" = "yes" ]; then
    stop
    rm -f "$PIDFILE"
    echo "Notice: log file was not removed: $LOGFILE" >&2
    update-rc.d -f $NAME remove
    rm -fv "$0"
  else
    echo "Abort!"
  fi

}

status() {
    printf "%-50s" "Checking $NAME ..."
    if [ -f $PIDFILE ] && [ -s $PIDFILE ]; then
        PID=$(cat $PIDFILE)
            if [ -z "$(ps axf | grep ${PID} | grep -v grep)" ]; then
                printf "%s\n" "The process appears to be dead but pidfile still exists"
            else
                echo "Running, the PID is $PID"
            fi
    else
        printf "%s\n" "Service not running"
    fi
}

update() {
    printf "%-50s" "building $NAME ..."
    stop
    echo "reload environment"
    source ~/.bash_profile
    echo "building with rethinkdb tags"
    go get -tags rethinkdb github.com/tinode/chat/server && go install -tags rethinkdb github.com/tinode/chat/server
    go get -tags rethinkdb github.com/tinode/chat/tinode-db && go install -tags rethinkdb github.com/tinode/chat/tinode-db
    echo "done updating "
    start
}

case "$1" in
  start)
    start
    ;;
  stop)
    stop
    ;;
  status)
    status
    ;;
  uninstall)
    uninstall
    ;;
  restart)
    stop
    start
    ;;
  update)
    update
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Usage: $0 {start|stop|status|restart|uninstall|update}"
esac
```

3. Copy this script in ```/etc/init.d/```
4. Make the script executable ```sudo chmod +x /etc/init.d/tinode```
5. Create log file ```sudo mkdir /var/log/tinode```
6. Ready to run it as service like this 
```sudo service tinode start```
```sudo service tinode stop```
```sudo service tinode restart```
```sudo service tinode update```

I don't have any real problem with this, but I think I'd like to roll it into a `parser.RelativeDeltaParser` class, not as an alternate constructor for `relativedelta`.

If it's going to be more general than tenors, though, the same issues that are dogging `parser.parser` come up - how to be open for enhancement such that users can expand if necessary.

I suggest the following thing:

1. Create a new `parser.RelativeDeltaParser` class.
2. Add a `parser._RelativeDeltaParserInfo` class that contains parser info, including a dictionary element mapping unit tokens to their appropriate unit.
3. Move this logic into a `RelativeDeltaParser.parse()` method, and utilize the dictionary mapping stuff in there. I think start with it being case-sensitive because that's what makes the most sense. You can always move the `.upper()` call to the input of the parser until we have a better handle on how to handle extension.

I'm not sure how the extension interface should look, which is why I think `_RelativeDeltaParserInfo` should be a private class and an instance of it attached only by default. So long as the public interface only exposes "call_some_func(some_string) -> relativedelta" and the tests only reflect things we *know* we already want to support, I don't see any problem expanding the API this little bit.

I think that would be quite confusing, because you can't just populate `tzname` - that's a *function* on `tzinfo`, so you'd have to attach a `tzinfo` with some constant `tzname` but presumably invalid values for DST and offset. `tzinfos` takes a dictionary or *callable*, though, so you can pass it a function that takes the `tzname` and returns a time zone.

Basically there are two goals with a recommender system -- the first is to provide an accurate prediction of the score (i.e. the error is RMSE), and the other is to recommend items that, with high confidence, have a high score but with which you don't really care about the actual predicted score.  There are a number of cases where using just a prediction of the score is bad -- e.g. an item has a single perfect rating, and the solution is to use a different objective that focuses on predicting only items that have a high score. These tend to perform much worse on RMSE errors but much better on Precision-Recall tasks.

Some more half-baked ideas to consider:
* Allow ``latitude`` and ``longitude`` to be optional arguments to a ``PVSystem`` object, but keep its ``Location`` inheritance.
* Make ``PVSystem`` a totally separate idea from a ``Location``. @bmu [discussed this](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/issues/17#issuecomment-95456673) earlier in the thread.
* Remove the ``Location --> PVSystem`` inheritance. Create a ``LocalizedPVSystem`` class that inherits from both ``Location`` and ``PVSystem``. A ``PVSystem`` object could be converted to a ``LocalizedPVSystem`` via an object method such as ``localize(latitude, longitude, altitude=0, timezone='UTC')`` or ``localize(location)``.

The diversity of opinions and use cases reinforces the need to keep the procedural code clean and flexible. Whatever object oriented API we ultimately go with will just be one possible solution.

> I agree, we could take this down into the stubs where ocaml can't look 
> into the code, so it won't collect anything.

The issue is not so much about C vs OCaml at the language level but at 
what the OCaml GC sees.  AFAIU, currently you store native pointers to 
Z3 objects directly into fields of ocaml record values.  The ocaml GC 
only knows that these are pointers since they are at least 2-aligned, 
and not to be followed by the gc since they point out of the ocaml heap. 
  These present some tricky cases for the gc, and note that since 4.02 
there is a configure-time option to disallow them and optimize the gc.

With custom blocks, the header tells the gc that there are no pointers 
to ocaml values in the block, which simplifies things.

But the main issue seems to be to keep the native pointer to a Z3 object 
from outliving the ocaml value that the finalizer is associated with.  
With naked pointers, this is hard, since ocaml code can separate the 
wrapper object (with the finalizer) from the native pointer just be 
getting a field.  With custom blocks, ocaml code cannot separate the 
native pointer from the ocaml value with the finalizer.  C code can, and 
so pointers to wrapper objects need to be protected from collection for 
the duration of C code that accesses the native pointer.  Since the gc 
only runs when the ocaml allocator is called, this protection is often 
pretty simple.  The CAMLparam macros are often enough.  If the native 
pointers are long-lived (e.g. across multiple api calls), then 
registering roots enters the picture.

One thing to keep in mind with registering roots is that this technique 
is often not enough to ensure ordering constraints such as ast 
finalizers are called before context finalizers.  In such cases you 
would need roots which are themselves in the gc heap.  The difficulty is 
that many objects may die in a collection pass, and their finalizers are 
called in arbitrary order.  So even if an ast wrapper has a pointer to a 
context, and that pointer is registered as a gc root, the ast and 
context wrappers can die in the same gc pass, and the context's 
finalizer can be called first.  This is for the C-level finalizers 
attached to custom blocks, the ocaml-level finalizers provided by the Gc 
module are rather heavier and provide a strong ordering guarantee.

> I kind of like the idea of using 
> caml_register_generational_global_root, but it's not particularly nice 
> either. Do you know whether that's compile-time and thus cheap or 
> runtime expensive?

Run time.  Registering a root adds the address to the set of addresses 
the gc starts scanning from.  I don't know if having lots of 
generational global roots is very expensive.  When I tried using them I 
ran into the ordering issues described above and had to switch to the 
ocaml Gc module finalizers instead.  I don't have the data anymore, but 
the version of the bindings that leak contexts was motivated by wanting 
to avoid that huge number of heavy-weight finalizers.  SLAyer chewed 
through way too many Z3 objects for that.  The cost was both run time as 
well as delaying when objects were collected, increasing memory 
consumption.

> Formally speaking, it's actually OK if OCaml collects the "high-level" 
> list-of-ast object. By creating a second copy of the native object, we 
> should increment the ref counts, because we do in fact hold another 
> reference, i.e., *_lton should actually call inf_ref on each object, 
> and we delete the list-of-native object after the function call (i.e., 
> call dec_ref on each).

Yes, there are two approaches that at some level are equivalent.  The 
custom block one prevents ocaml code from being able to make copies of 
native pointers, and thereby ensures that the native pointer does not 
live past the finalizer call.  Another approach is to allow ocaml code 
to make copies, but require it to call inc_ref and dec_ref manually.

> We could simply introduce a new data structure which calls the dec_ref 
> for each element when the list/array goes out of scope (e.g., put a 
> finalizer onto the array?), but OCaml users seem not to be partial to 
> object-orientation it seems.

Yeah, we have a real module system.

> As Martin said, this will also create loads of API calls which isn't 
> particularly attractive either.
>
> Originally the idea was to create a new luxury, highest-level 
> structure as in the other APIs, for OCaml without the need for 
> camlidl. But, almost nothing of that idea remains, so perhaps it's 
> best if we just revert the native layer to what camlidl used to 
> generate, and perhaps even use it, if it comes with all the different 
> ocaml flavours now.

Flavors?  The code it generates uses type names defined in the ocaml 
runtime headers, so they automatically adjust to e.g. word size with the 
runtime system.

Still, with the code camlidl generates as a guide, it is hopefully not 
too big a job to adjust the Z3 generator to match.  The main point where 
the approaches differ that is important here, IIRC, is that camlidl 
allows defining per-C-type conversions between OCaml and C values in C.  
I don't know how big the consequences would be of changing to handle 
reference counting in the C code rather than having an ultimately-thin C 
layer and doing everything else in ocaml.

> We could still keep the module structure in z3.ml/mli which people 
> seem to like.

This should be configurable via the `shared_axes` argument, so people can configure it the way they want. You'd do something like:
```python
shape = list(input_shape)
for ax in self.shared_axes:
    shape[ax] = 1
rnd = self._srng.uniform(shape)
rnd = theano.tensor.addbroadcastable(rnd, *self.shared_axes)
return theano.tensor.nnet.relu(input, rnd)
```

after some debugging, I found this is caused by brk simulation:
the program called sbrk with arg 0x11c0:

.text:0000000000410D4C                 call    sbrk
.text:0000000000410D51                 lea     rsi, [rax+rbx-1]

but rax  ==  <SAO <BV64 0xffffffff>> means sbrk failed.

this is caused by (now rdi  ==  <SAO <BV64 0x1b011c0>>):
.text:000000000044EE70 loc_44EE70:                             ; CODE XREF: sbrk+32j
.text:000000000044EE70                 lea     rdi, [rbx+rbp]
.text:000000000044EE74                 call    brk
.text:000000000044EE79                 test    eax, eax
.text:000000000044EE7B                 js      short loc_44EE42

with 
ip  ==  <SAO <BV64 0x44ee79>>
rax  ==  <SAO <BV64 0xffffffff>>

so obviously brk failed.

in source code I noticed:
            # failure cases: new brk is less than old brk, or new brk is not page aligned            
            if conc_end < conc_start or conc_end % 0x1000 != 0:
                pass
            else:
                # TODO: figure out what permissions to use
                self.state.memory.map_region(conc_start, conc_end - conc_start, 7)
                self.brk = new_brk

conc_end = 0x1b011c0 so conc_end % 0x1000 != 0 and this caused the failure....

but on real system not aligned brk is ok. so maybe the fix is just to align it instead of failure?
conc_end = (conc_end + 0xfff) & ~0xfff

Clarify: `CFGAccurate` does not perform a linear scan to cover the entire address space. Its CFG recovery algorithm is purely based on connectivity, which is to say, it shouldn't include code blocks that are not reachable* from your starting point.

\* `CFGAccurate` performs forced execution to follow all branches, so unsatisfiable branches are still considered as "reachable".

```
def get_polygon_area(corners):
    """Get the area of the convex area defined by *corners*.
    """
    # We assume the earth is spherical !!!
    # Should be the radius of the earth at the observed position
    R = 1

    c1_ = corners[0]
    area = 0

    for idx in range(1, len(corners) - 1):
        b1_ = Arc(c1_, corners[idx])
        b2_ = Arc(c1_, corners[idx + 1])
        b3_ = Arc(corners[idx], corners[idx + 1])
        e__ = (abs(b1_.angle(b2_)) +
               abs(b2_.angle(b3_)) +
               abs(b3_.angle(b1_)))
        area += e__ - math.pi
    return R**2 * area
```

I solved my issue, it seems the problem was because of missing `backref ` parameters in  the SQLAlchemy relationships I created for these two entities. Defining the SQLAlchemy classes as below solved my issue.

```
from .base import Base, Dictionary
from .status import Status
from sqlalchemy import Column, DateTime, ForeignKey, Integer, String
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship
from sqlalchemy.sql import func


class BatchOwner(Base, Dictionary):
    """Batch owners."""

    __tablename__ = 'batch_owner'

    id = Column('batch_owner_id', Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column('batch_owner', String, nullable=False, unique=True)
    createdDate = Column('created_date', DateTime, server_default=func.now())
    updatedDate = Column('updated_date', DateTime, server_default=func.now(), onupdate=func.now())

    batches = relationship('Batch', backref='batchOwner', passive_deletes=True, lazy='subquery')
    indicators = relationship('Indicator', backref='batchOwner')


class Batch(Base, Dictionary):
    """Batches."""

    __tablename__ = 'batch'

    id = Column('batch_id', Integer, primary_key=True)
    batchOwnerId = Column('batch_owner_id', Integer, ForeignKey('batch_owner.batch_owner_id', ondelete='CASCADE'), nullable=False)
    statusId = Column('status_id', Integer, ForeignKey('status.status_id'), nullable=False)
    createdDate = Column('created_date', DateTime, server_default=func.now())
    updatedDate = Column('updated_date', DateTime, server_default=func.now(), onupdate=func.now())

    sessions = relationship('Session', backref='batch', passive_deletes=True)
```

@Fedalto Thanks for the feedback.

It would be nice if we could shift the responsibility for name mangling to Graphene. But we can't do that since Graphene is a lower level interface and therefore does not and should not care. It takes the Enum type names and options exactly like you define them. This allows you to build anything with it. Note that while Graphene *does* in fact convert field names automatically, you can still override it by explicitly setting names on the fields.

However, Graphene-SQLAlchemy is a higher level interface that seeks to "translate" from SQL to GraphQL in a conventient and automatic fashion. Therefore, in my view, it should - at least by default - also translate the naming conventions, which are lowercase enum options and type names in SQL, but uppercase enum options and camel case type names in GraphQL. That way you get what you normally expect in each of the two worlds instead of forcing an alien naming convention to the other world.

Note that Graphene-SQLAlchemy also converts field names from snake_case to camelCase using the default conversion mechanism in Graphene, but contrary to when you use Graphene manually, you can't change that behavior - since the fields are created automatically, you have no chance to override the name parameter in that automatic process.

Of course, it would be nice if we could make the automatic conversion configurable on the level of the registry, class or field. On the class level, maybe we could use the Meta attribute, like this?
```python
class UserType(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = User
        translate_field_names = True
        translate_enum_options = False
        translate_type_names = True
```

The last comment I have here is, this PR is getting ugly big... *144* files now!

The two big changes that cause this count are:

a)  Many "cpp" files do not include <openthread-config.h>
These files then go on - and include other openthread headers
Which ... in turn need or require DEFINE from openthread-config.h

I did not fix all of these, more work needs to be done here.
In some cases, the config file is included *LATE* after other header files have been processed.

Because that (config.h) define is NOT defined, the C-Preprocessor rules state the value is ZERO
When in fact - in the openthread-config.h file, the value is 1.

Thus in many many cases, I had to add  an include <openthread-config.h>
or move this to the top of list of includes.

In some cases, for example: "cli.hpp" - includes "openthread-config.h" however
in some cases, the source file has *ALREADY* included other headers.

SUGGESTION:  I think some type of 'missing-include-guard' needs to be implemented in some key header files.

b) Lots of:  <string.h> becomes "utils/wrap_string.h"
Likewise for others - with no other change (or in addition to including openthread-config.h)

c) There's an ugly problem with windows here that comes in a few parts.

Part 1:

Currently Windows has a *SINGULAR* configuration file.
When in fact I think it really needs two different config files.

For example, when building a windows application the files: "stdint.h" are present (these are built using VS-2015 which *IS* c99 compliant)

And thus - the windows-config file *SHOULD* have  HAVE_STDINT_H = 1 (likewise for others)
But when building KERNEL this file is not available, so the config file must have HAVE_STDINT_H=0

My solution was this: Hoist these two defines into the VCXPROJ files as comand line defines.

Having a single config.h file is very anti-auto-configure.

SUGGESTION: I think this should be addressed in a different PR.

Part 2:

Openthread public header files *REQUIRE* the file: stdint.h to exist, in both Kernel and App builds.
So - I can add a replacement 'stdint.h' - but - it should only apply if and only if compiling KERNEL Mode

I cannot (should not) put this replacement file in a "common windows include directory" (as @nibanks) suggested . Reason:  When building the application - we should really get the compilers <stdint.h> and not the replacement file.

Hence, I created a  "include_c99" directory where @nibanks suggested,  and *ONLY* in the Kernel build vcxproj files - add this directory to the list of include paths.

**END**

Anymail uses the [Python Requests](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/) package for communication with most ESPs, including Mandrill. There's no code in Anymail specific to TLS versions. I believe Requests uses the best version of TLS available to Python (and supported by the remote server), and TLS 1.2 is available in any reasonably-up-to-date configuration.

So if you're seeing API calls using TLS 1.0 or 1.1, the most likely cause is an outdated Python and/or OpenSSL on your system. Look for "to check your Python interpreter's TLS version" in [this post](http://pyfound.blogspot.com/2017/01/time-to-upgrade-your-python-tls-v12.html). Assuming that's the problem, upgrading Python and OpenSSL should solve it—or look for "python TLS 1.2 *your-platform*" in your favorite search engine. (If you're running Python older than 2.7.9, you should *definitely* upgrade for lots of related reasons.)

If that doesn't help, we'd need to know what OS and version, what Python version, what OpenSSL version, and what version of Requests you have installed.

* Some kind of `--uid` flag to set the user id for all of `/etc/letsencrypt/`

So what `--noninteractive` does is ensure the client doesn't hang waiting for user input. It does not automatically answer questions for you, it will error out when it encounters a prompt, telling you how to  specify the answer to that prompt on the command line. This is useful when running the client automatically using something like `cron` so it will not hang forever waiting for user input.

Here, the client didn't know whether you wanted to expand the existing cert or to create an entirely separate one. If you want to expand the existing cert, include `--expand` on the command line. If you want to create a new lineage, use `--duplicate` (although you probably don't want to use this for every renewal, as it will create new directories in `/etc/letsencrypt` on every run).

If I'm incorrect about the issue you're reporting, please let me know, however, I believe this is just a misunderstanding of the purpose of `--noninteractive`.

The issue is that SCIP has different stages. For instance, when you just create a SCIP you are in INIT, then when you create a problem you are in PROB, then when you presolve, you are in PRESOLVING, then in SOLVING, etc.

In general, most function can only be called in some stages. In the documentation of `SCIPtrySol` you will see (something like)
```
 *  @pre This method can be called if SCIP is in one of the following stages:
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_TRANSFORMED
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_INITPRESOLVE
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_PRESOLVING
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_EXITPRESOLVE
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_PRESOLVED
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_SOLVING
```

While the stages of `SCIPaddSol` are
```
 *  @pre This method can be called if SCIP is in one of the following stages:
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_PROBLEM
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_TRANSFORMED
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_INITPRESOLVE
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_PRESOLVING
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_EXITPRESOLVE
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_PRESOLVED
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_SOLVING
 *       - \ref SCIP_STAGE_FREETRANS
```

So you see, `trySol` will not work in the problem stage, which is where you are when you set up a model in PySCIPOpt

Hi,

First of all, I'm glad you've taken the time to check my benchmarks and to try to come up with better competition to Lark. I'll address your points in the order you presented them:

I'm not sure it makes sense to measure only the tree construction, any more than it makes sense to measure only the lexer. A parser is a complete product, especially Lark & Parsimonious which offer more than just a parsing engine (and if you want just a *fast engine*, there are C++/Haskell/Rust parsers that I can recommend).

It is interesting to learn that Parsimonious could be faster. Perhaps they should adopt a tree-less option like I have in Lark.

Likewise, Lark makes no claims to beat a parser that's handwritten in Cython. Perhaps a more fair comparison would be against Lark running on PyPy, rather than CPython. I do have plans to write a version of Lark in Cython, but that might take a while. I'll welcome your help, since you seem to know Cython well. Perhaps you can share the Cython parser you already wrote, so I can learn from your experience.

> It could be that my use case of parsing a small JSON object many times instead of one giant one has a different performance profile

That is definitely the case. Because LALR(1) has a non-trivial compilation time, and linear performance, it really shines on long inputs.  If you must test it on shorter inputs, consider using the standalone parser, initializes much faster than the library.

Finally, I benchmarked your parser, on the same 6MB json file I did the other benchmarks. It ran for 15 seconds (better than the previous 20 seconds), and quit with the following error:

    IncompleteParseError: Rule 'Start' matched in its entirety, but it didn't consume all the text. The non-matching portion of the text begins with '
' (line 225002, column 2).

If you wish to fix the parser and resubmit it, I'll be happy to try it again. I can also provide you with the "offending" json, if you need it.

One little note though: Even if Lark had lost on performance (though I'd definitely say that's not the case), I think writing constructs such as `~"\s*:\s*"` just to gain a few percents might not be worth it, even if you know what you're doing. And definitely not if you're a newbie to parsing, which is a big chunk of Lark's audience.

Thanks again for your input!
